items.q: How does the sign of cross elasticity of demand (XED) reveal product relationships?
items.correct: Positive XED = substitutes (price of one rises, demand for the other rises); negative XED = complements (price of one rises, demand for the other falls); zero XED = unrelated goods.
items.misconception: The sign of XED doesn't matter — only the size does.
items.random_wrong: XED measures how often consumers cross-shop between stores.
items.why: The sign carries the qualitative information about the relationship — substitute vs complement. Size tells you the strength of the relationship. Students focus on magnitude and miss the categorical meaning of the sign.

items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: What defines a pure public good?
items.correct: Non-rivalry (one person's use doesn't reduce others') and non-excludability (you can't stop people benefiting) — e.g. national defence, street lighting.
items.misconception: A public good is anything provided by the government.
items.random_wrong: A public good is anything that's free.
items.why: Government provides many private goods (e.g. NHS treatment is rival). The economic definition is about the GOOD's properties, not who provides it. Students confuse provider with technical category.
Correct answer: Money in. “Money out” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Bigger than income. “Less than income” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
